coredata.dc:title: CQD@γ-Fe2O3 multifunctional nanoprobe for selective fluorescence sensing, detoxification and removal of Hg(II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Panda, Snigdharani
Paital, Biswaranjan
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: In this paper we have developed a simple integrated nanoprobe that combines fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) and superparamagnetic magnetic iron oxide(SPION) equipped with –SH, -COOH functionalization for sensing and removal of mercury from aqueous medium. The probe has diminished fluorescence due to photoelectron transfer between CQD fluorophore and magnetic nanoparticle. A remarkable enhancement in fluorescence emission intensity is achieved in presence of Hg2+ which inhibits the PET mechanism. The limit of detection of this fluorescence turn on sensing is 0.376 nM which is the lowest among all reported protocol for Hg sensing. Due to presence of abundant functional groups such as –SH, -COOH in the magnetic component the probe shows excellent mercury removal efficiency of 70%. The utility of the probe has also been explored to monitor Hg in bacteria as well as in living fish sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Hg
Turn-on sensing
Fluorescence imaging
In vivosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of DMSA functionalized iron nanoparticle
Measurement of Hg recovery in water samples
Hg sensing experiment in bacteria cells
Fluorescence Response of probe in presence of Hg
Binding of Hg to Fe2O3-DMSA probe
Hg detection in bacteria cells and fish samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Luminescence sensing mechanism of Hg using CQD-γ-Fe2O3 integrated nanoprobe.
Illustration showing magnetic removal of Hg.
(a) TEM (b) HRTEM image of EDBE modified CQD, (c–f) STEM elemental mapping showing the presence of C, O, N.
FTIR spectra of CQD and EDBE modified CQD.

(a) UV–vis spectra of CQD and amine modified CQD, (b) Excitation dependent Emission of CQD-EDBE (λex300-340nm→ λem 422 nm and λex 360, 380, 400,420, 440, 460, 480 nm corresponds to λem 444, 452, 467, 481, 494, 509, 546 nm respectively).
Nitrogen adsorption- desorption isotherm of Fe2O3-DMSA.
FTIR comparison spectra of Fe2O3 and Fe2O3-DMSA.
(a) Optimization of Fe2O3 concentration in nanoprobe, (b) PL quenching of CQD-EDBE as a function of Fe2O3 concentration.
(a)TEM image of integrated nanoprobe, (b) HRTEM image showing lattice fringes.
Comparison of zeta potential of CQD-EDBE, Fe2O3-DMSA, CQD-γFe2O3.
(a)PL response of CQD-γFe2O3 probe towards Hg, (b)linear response,(c) Selectivity of probe towards Hg, (d)Response towards Hg in presence of other cations.
Impact of pH on sensing Hg2+.
Fluorescence lifetime of CQD-EDBE, CQD-γFe2O3, and CQD-γFe2O3-Hg2+.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) High resolution scan of, (c) O1s, (d) S2p, (e) Hg4f, (f) Fe2p.
Field dependent magnetization curve of γ-Fe2O3-DMSA.
cell viability of our probe (CQD-γFe2O3).
The fluorescence images of (a) S. Aureus, (c) E. Coli stained with CQD-γFe2O3under excitation 405 and 488 nm, (b, d) after addition of Hg to the stained respective bacterial cells.

Confocal imaging of muscle, gill, liver, brain stained with CQD-γFe2O3(a) bright field image, (b) image under 405 nm excitation, after addition of Hg2+(c) bright field image, (d) image under 405 nm excitation to the respective body parts stained with probe.
Mercury removal efficiency of probe assayed using AAS technique.
Analytical results of Hg found in fish organs.

head.ce:title: CQD@γ-Fe2O3 multifunctional nanoprobe for selective fluorescence sensing, detoxification and removal of Hg(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, 769008, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, 769008, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Redox Regulation Laboratory, College of Basic Sciences and Humanities, Orissa University of Agriculture and Technology, Bhubaneswar, 751003 India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Basic Sciences and Humanities
Orissa University of Agriculture and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bRedox Regulation Laboratory, College of Basic Sciences and Humanities, Orissa University of Agriculture and Technology, Bhubaneswar, 751003 India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper we have developed a simple integrated nanoprobe that combines fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) and superparamagnetic magnetic iron oxide(SPION) equipped with –SH, -COOH functionalization for sensing and removal of mercury from aqueous medium. The probe has diminished fluorescence due to photoelectron transfer between CQD fluorophore and magnetic nanoparticle. A remarkable enhancement in fluorescence emission intensity is achieved in presence of Hg2+ which inhibits the PET mechanism. The limit of detection of this fluorescence turn on sensing is 0.376 nM which is the lowest among all reported protocol for Hg sensing. Due to presence of abundant functional groups such as –SH, -COOH in the magnetic component the probe shows excellent mercury removal efficiency of 70%. The utility of the probe has also been explored to monitor Hg in bacteria as well as in living fish sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is extensively exposed to environment by fossil fuel burning, chemical manufacturing, coal and gold mining, oil refining, mercury lamps and from fertilizer industries [1,2]. Accumulation of this toxic metal ion in human and animal body leads to cell abnormalities and results diseases like kidney failure, damage to the CNS system, impairs mitosis, cognitive and motion disorders [3,4]. Hg forms chelates with the functional group like mercapto, carboxyl, carbonyl and amino which significantly affects antioxidant proteins or enzymes [5,6]. Due to this kind of venomous effect of mercury, the Environmental Protection Agency has fixed 2 μg/L as the maximum acceptable concentration of mercury contamination in drinking water [7]. Among the many trustworthy techniques for the quantification of mercury ions atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [10] and X- ray absorption spectroscopy [11] are commonly used for detection of Hg. Although these methods are sensitive and accurate but these are not suitable for on field detection due to lack of portability, high expenditure, fine controlled experimental condition and involvement of expert technician. In this regard, a variety of powerful sensors such as DNA based sensors [12], electrochemical sensors [13], colorimetric sensors [14] and fluorometric sensors [15,16] have attracted considerable attention for the detection of mercury ions. Out of them fluorometric method has been widely used for real time monitoring for Hg in biological and environmental samples as it is non-destructive to sample and least harmful to the cell. So far many fluorescent probes such as traditional dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, DNA-aptamers, gold nanoparticles, organic fluorophore have been developed for the detection of mercury ion [17–22]. However, drawbacks such as lower photostability, higher toxicity, less water solubility and high cost limit their practical application. Interestingly, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) with its distinctive features such as excellent photostability, water dispersibility, easy functionalization, biocompatibility and affordable cost etc. have gathered a great attention of researchers in this particular field [23–25]. This unique photoluminescence property enabled CQD as a sensor for broad range of cations and anions [26–28]. The present author has also reported selective detection of F, Hg, glucosamine, atrazine and flumioxazin [29–33] using CQD based fluorescent nanosensor. Recently, G. Gnana et al. have reported the application of N doped carbon dot derived from citron fruit extract and urine for the selective detection of Hg and cysteine [34]. S. H. Hasan et al. synthesized green fluorescent label free carbon dots using Tamarindus indica leaves. The fluorescence intensity at 433 nm gradually decreases upon addition of Hg and reappears in the presence of glutathione biomolecule [35]. Though these methods are helpful in detection of mercury, most of them are based on turn-off statergy. Fluorescence turn-on sensor it more preferable than a turn-off sensor as it enables small amounts of light to be measured relative to a dark background. Furthermore, removal of Hg is a big challenge because it rapidly spread into large areas because of its high mobility. Hence, inspired by the surface-assimilative property of iron oxide nanoparticle (IONP) [36] and contemplating the above-mentioned advantage of highly fluorescent CQD, we envision to bring both the optical and magnetic properties of nanoparticle into a single platform for both sensing and removal of mercury ion. The advantages of iron oxide-carbon quantum dot integrated nanoparticles have been explored in the field of imaging, catalytic activity, drug release and photothermal therapy. To the best of our knowledge, till now integrated nanoparticles are not used in fluorescence sensing and elimination of heavy metal ions.

In this paper we report the design a dual consolidative nanoprobe consisting of luminescent 2,2′-(Ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) modified CQD(CQD-EDBE) and dimercapto-succinic acid modified maghemite (γFe2O3-DMSA) that will not only detect Hg2+ but also manifest the ability to extract mercury ions. As outlined in Scheme 1 , the intense green colour of CQD-EDBE is suppressed in presence of γFe2O3-DMSA due to photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from CQD to γFe2O3 and a non fluorescencent CQD- γFe2O3 probe is formed. Upon addition of Hg, the γFe2O3-DMSA moiety of CQD-γFe2O3 nanoprobe binds with Hg through its thiol and carboxyl groups leaving behind the aminated CQD. Eventually, this enables remarkable enhancement of fluorescence emission intensity of CQD-EDBE along with removal of Hg using an external bar magnet.

FeCl3.6H2O and FeCl2.4H2O were purchased from SRL chemicals. Fe(NO3)3.9H2O, Ethanol was procured from MERCK chemicals. H2SO4, HCl, HNO3, NH4OH, CuSO4.5H2O and ascorbic acid were supplied by SDFCL. 2,2′-(Ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine), deionized water, HEPES were brought from Sigma Aldrich. DMSA, Hg(NO3)2. H2O, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, Mg(NO3)2.6H2O, Ba(NO3)2, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, NiCl2, were purchased from Himedia chemicals. DCM, sucrose was supplied by fisher scientific chemicals.

The luminescent CQDs were synthesized using sucrose and ascorbic acid as precursors via one-step hydrothermal carbonisation process. Briefly, Sucrose and ascorbic acid (2:1) were solubilized in a 70 mL mixture of millipore water and ethanol. Catalytic quantity of 1 M H2SO4 was added to boost the rate of reaction. Then, the transparent solution was poured in a Teflon lined autoclave and carbonised for 3 h at 140 °C. After completion of reaction the crude solution was repeatedly washed with DCM to remove partially carbonised impurities. Then, the solution was treated with excess acetone and centrifuged at 30,000 rpm to remove large particles. Then, the solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 1 day. Finally, the obtained yellow coloured solution was freeze-dried to get CQD powders. Further, 100 mg of CQD powder was stirred with 200 μL of EDBE in 20 mL deionized water for 24 h at RT to obtain amine functionalized CQD. The obtained product was again washed with excess acetone followed by dialysis to remove residual EDBE. The synthesized CQD-EDBE was brought to neutral pH upon the addition of few drops of HCl. Again CQD-EDBE was lyophilized and redispersed in HEPES buffer for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of DMSA functionalized iron nanoparticle
ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic γ-Fe2O3 (maghemite) nanoparticles were synthesized according to a previously reported literature [37]. (see details in supplementary information)

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Horiba Quantamaster fluorimeter. UV–vis absorption spectra were taken using UV probe 2450, Shimadzu. Morphological and microstructural study and elemental charting were conducted using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Technai G2 30ST) operated at 300 kV. The presence of the surface functionality was investigated through FTIR spectroscopy conducted using an IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu, spectrophotometer. The surface interaction of Hg with CQD-γFe2O3 was verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)using an Al Kα excitation source in the PHI 5000 Versa Probe II (FEI Inc.) instrument. The identification of crystalline phase of maghemite particles was performed by an Expert Pro Phillips X-ray diffractometer. The hydrodynamic size and surface charge at different pH values were measured via Malvern Nano ZS90. SQUID-VSM instrument (Evercool SQUID VSM DC Magnetometer) is used to analyze the superparamagnetic behavior of γFe2O3-DMSA nanoparticle at room temperature.

The sensing of Hg was conducted in deionized water, tape water, river water and HEPES buffer solution. Typically, 100 μL of (5 mg/mL) CQD-EDBE, 200 μL of Fe2O3-DMSA was mixed in HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) to develop the CQD-γFe2O3 nanoprobe. Later on, 10 μL of Hg with different concentration (0.025 μM–2.25 μM) were mixed and incubated for 45 min at room temperature before PL emission measurement. The PL spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm after completion of incubation period. We have followed the same controlled procedure so as to investigate the selectivity of our probe towards Hg over several cations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of Hg recovery in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the usefulness of this fluorescence assay, Tape water and Koel river water were used for Hg detection. All these samples were centrifuged and filtered with 0.2 μm membranes. The obtained filtrate was spiked with few different concentrations of Hg samples and the PL spectra were noted considering the same protocol.

Toxicity of our synthesized CQD-γFe2O3nanoprobe was examined against two potential bacteria E. coli and S. aureus. Briefly, the growth kinetics of both the bacteria was evaluated by adding varying concentration of CQD-EDBE, Fe2O3-DMSA, and integrated CQD-γFe2O3(50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL) in a 96 well plate having 200 μL final volume media in each well with overnight culture. The growth of bacteria was monitored in a multi-mode plate reader at an optical density of 600 nm at regular interval for 16 hs at 37 °C. The experiments were made thrice under same condition and graph was plotted with optical density versus time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg sensing experiment in bacteria cells
ce:section.ce:para: Both gram-positive and negative microbes were matured in LB and NB medium in 12 h at 37⁰C individually. Bacterial stain was gathered from each colony to measure its optical density which is close to 1.0. The bacterial cells were purified and centrifuged 2 times with sterilized PBS at 12000 rpm for 10 min again resuspended in 20 mL PBS. Three test tubes of E. coli suspension each having 5 ml were prepared. First aliquot was kept as control, both second, third aliquot was treated with 100 μL of each Fe2O3-DMSA and CQD-EDBE (1 mg mL−1) and incubated for 3 h whereas only the third one was again exposed with10 μL of Hg (1 μM) and incubated for 2 h at 37⁰C. Each treated sample were centrifuged and washed repeatedly with PBS to remove residual probe and analyte. The pellet was dispersed in PBS buffer. From each set, sample was spread over glass slides and confocal micro-scope (Leica TCS SP8) images were captured. The fluorescent images were taken upon excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm.

We have also tested the applicability of our probe inside live small fish (Aplocheilus panchax). In order to avoid contamination, fish samples were stacked from a place which is far from industry, human abode. All fish were attuned in laboratory environment and healthy fish (N = 30 fishes) having body weight 0.1 gm to 0.3 gm were chosen for the experiment. The fish were cultured in three sets. The first set (containing 3 fishes) was maintained in pond water and considered as control. 10 μL (5 mg/mL) of our CQD-γFe2O3 nanoprobe was injected to the fishes of second and third set through Hamilton syringe. These are allowed to stand in pond water for 3 h. Then the third set of fish was fed with 50 μM of Hg in another container for 1 h. All the sets of containers were aerated. After 1 h, though these fishes of third set were alive but mostly moribund due to presence of Hg. Each fish was rinsed with PBS buffer two times to remove the remaining probe and analyte from its surface. Each fish was dissected well and its body parts such as gills (exposed to outside), muscle (metabolically less active tissue), brain (metabolically active tissue) and liver (metabolically most active tissue) collected then cleansed with PBS buffer. Then the body parts of each set were placed over glass slides and subjected to confocal for imaging.

Uniform CQDs were generated through hydrothermal synthetic route using ascorbic acid, sucrose as molecular precursors. Initially surface of CQD contains –COOH, -OH and epoxy groups. Later 2,2′-(Ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) is used as a surface functionalizing as well as passivating agent producing highly luminescent amine terminated CQD. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image shown in Fig. 1 a confirmed the presence of smaller particle with average diameter 4.5–6.5 nm. A high resolution TEM revealed the d-spacing of 0.37 nm which is in harmony with the basal plane of graphite (Fig. 1b). STEM-EDAX specified in Fig. 1c shown that CQD-EDBE composed of C, N, O and elemental mapping evidenced the uniform distribution of these elements throughout the surface.

Surface functional moieties such as CO, CC, COH, COC, OH, CN, NH of CQD-EDBE was confirmed by FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ). Amine and hydroxyl groups were confirmed from broad absorption bands at 2900−3400 cm−1. The peaks corresponding to CC, CO of (CHO, COOH, CONH) are noticed in the wide range of 1500-1750 cm−1. Signal intensified in the range of 1000- 1400 cm−1evidenced CC, COC and CN functional groups. The presence of COOH, OH and NH2 groups imparts excellent stability to the particle in aqueous medium.

Pristine CQD has absorption band at 222 and 284 nm which is attributed to π-π* transition raised from aromatic sp2 domains. CQD-EDBE shows an additional band at 345 nm in addition to band at 222 and 284 nm (Fig. 3 a) which is attributable to n-π* transition (heteroatom N, O bond) of amine functionalized CQD. When the excitation wavelength of CQD-EDBE is changed from 360 to 480 nm, a regular bathochromic shift of peaks is seen. This tunable emission under different excitation wavelength is an exclusive property of CQDs and which makes CQD an excellent imaging agent.

The addition of Fe2O3-DMSA into CQD-EDBE solution in HEPES buffer medium led to PL quenching of CQD-EDBE (ƛex=360 nm). The PL intensity of CQD-EDBE gradually declines with Fe2O3-DMSAin concentration range 10 μL–300 μL (1 mg/ml) (fig.6 a). F/F0 of CQD-EDBE vs. Fe2O3-DMSA concentration shows linear fit in fig.6b with correlation coefficient of 0.975. Fluorescence quenching may be attributed to electron transition from the excited states of CQD-EDBE to unoccupied d-orbital of Fe3+ in γ-Fe2O3. However, the fluorescence intensity of nanoprobe was recovered on the addition of Hg. The formation of integrated nanohybrid CQD-γFe2O3 which leads to suppression of PL intensity of CQD-EDBE is evidenced from TEM, XPS and fluorescence lifetime measurement. Fig.7 a shows typical TEM image of CQD-γFe2O3 which demonstrates sphere like morphology. It is observed that each spherical assembly has an average diameter 12−14 nm, reflecting the strong interaction between aminated CQD (CQD-EDBE) and thiolated Fe2O3 (Fe2O3-DMSA). High resolution TEM image shows different fringe pattern with d-spacing 0.31 and 0.22 nm corresponding to [113] plane of CD and [101] plane of Fe2O3 respectively (Fig. 7b). The binding between CQD-EDBE and Fe2O3-DMSA is further supported by remarkable change in zeta potential from -9.41 mV of CQD-EDBE to -14.5 mV of CQD-γFe2O3integrated probe (Fig. 8 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Response of probe in presence of Hg
ce:section.ce:para: At optimum condition the fluorescence response of CQD-γFe2O3 probe towards Hg was investigated. Fig.9 a clearly indicated the substantial enhancement in PL emission intensity of CQD-γFe2O3 (at λem 440 nm/ λex 360 nm) with regard to Hg concentration. The uplifted fluorescence emission intensity is in a linear relationship with Hg concentration, R2 = 0.989 over an extensive concentration series of 0–2.25 μM (Fig. 9b).

The limit of detection was predicted from the statistical treatment of the calibration curve [38]. The regression data facilitated the calculation of standard error of intercept followed by standard deviation of intercept. Then using formula 3.3*(SD of intercept/ slope), LOD of this sensor was computed to be 0.376 nM. We have also verified PL response time of CQD-γFe2O3 towards Hg and observed the maximum intensity after 45 min, reaches plateau after 1 h.

To ensure on-target effect of CQD-γFe2O3 towards Hg, few photoluminescence screening experiments were carried out. As displayed in Fig. 9c, though a number of common metal ions such as Na, K, Mg, Ca, Ni, Zn, Ba, Cu, Cd, Pb along with Hg were added to the sensing system, but this probe selectively shown outstanding change in PL intensity towards of Hg. However, other metal ions demonstrated insignificant effects on the luminescence of CQDs. Therefore, with a sharp change in fluorescence intensity “off-on” nanoprobe was successfully constructed for accurate monitoring of Hg. In fig.9d, Interference challenges of Hg alone and a mixture of above-mentioned metal ions along with Hg were evaluated. Result obtained from PL spectra proved negligible influence of other metal ions. Hence our probe is not selective and sensitive towards other metal ions. pH is an important parameter which influences the intensity of emission band of CQD-γFe2O3 on addition of Hg2+. In order to explore the working pH range of the developed sensor the emission intensity at 450 nm was observed for a particular concentration of Hg2+ with in the pH range 4–8 (Fig. 10 ). It was found that at pH 7.4 the enhancement in emission intensity of CQD-γFe2O3 is maximum whereas at acidic pH there is remarkably less increase in intensity which may be due to competition between H+ and Hg2+ for thiol group, hence resulting less adsorption of Hg2+ on DMSA@γFe2O3. At alkaline pH the sensing is not well carried out because of precipitation of Hg(OH)2.

It is well known that CQD can perform as both electron donor and acceptor in the excited state through photo-induced electron transfer mechanism. Fig. 6a depicts the decrease in PL intensity of CQD-EDBE with an increase in concentration of thiolated iron nanoparticle. Fluorescence quenching took place due to the transfer of excited state from the CQD-EDBE to empty d-orbital of Fe3+in γ-Fe2O3 [39]. To gain further insight into the PL quenching mechanism, we investigated exciton behaviour of CQD-EDBE (at emission intensity maxima at λem=440 nm) in presence and absence of iron oxide nanoparticle using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique [40]. The data were found to be fit well to a classical three-exponential function (Table S1). According to TCSPC results, the average life time of amine terminated CQD was found to be 1.8 ns, whereas CQD-EDBE on after addition of Fe2O3-DMSA shows an average lifetime 0.3 ns. This lifetime results provide influential proof in support of our formerly mentioned opinion. According to the above analysis the fluorescence quenching of CQD-EDBE ensued due to the dynamic photo-induced electron transfer that arose from complexation of CQD-EDBE and Fe2O3-DMSA. Again, incubation of the probe with Hg results fluorescence enhancement of CQD-EDBE. The average lifetime of probe owing to Hg was found to be 0.6 ns (Fig. 11 ). This could be explained as follows. DMSA functionalized Fe2O3 facilitates free –SH and −COOH groups on its surface which has a strong affinity towards Hg [41]. Presence of Hg restricted the electrostatic interaction between CQD and Iron nanoparticle, DMSA modified iron nanoparticle binds strongly to Hg analyte leaving CQD aside. This results the fluorescence recovery of CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding of Hg to Fe2O3-DMSA probe
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the above anticipated mechanism further XPS analysis of CQD-γFe2O3 nanoprobe after addition with Hg was carried out. Fig. 12 (a–e) has given insight to the existence of different chemical moieties on the exterior of nanoprobe. The peaks in the survey spectrum at 98–106, 282–289, 397–402, 527–533, 160–165, 706−728 eV resembles to the binding energies of Hg4f, O1s, S2p, Fe2p3/2 respectively. The C1s binding energy related to the reference 284 eV shows 1 eV shift to the lower binding energy. Accordingly, binding energies of other groups are calculated. The high-resolution scan of O1s corresponding to Hg-O binding energy at 528.7 eV, CO at 530.1 and 531.4 eV, CO at 532.5 eV. The high resolution S2p photoemission is fitted to three peaks at 161, 162.4, 163.8 eV. The binding energy 161.1, 162.3 eV corresponds to Hg-S/ Fe-S bond, 163.8 eV binding energy corresponds to surface functional group –SH / −COSH. The Fe2p region shows four peaks at 709.3, 711.4, 716.7, 723.5 eV which is in harmony with the Fe2p binding energy for γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles. The binding energy of Hg 4f7/2 at 99.91 eV and Hg 4f5/2 at 103.8 eV corresponds to the HgS, oxidized mercury respectively.

Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) was used to inspect the magnetic property of γ-Fe2O3-DMSA with the magnetic field cycle between -15 and +15 KOe at ambient temperature. Fig.13 elucidated the increase in magnetization value of γ-Fe2O3-DMSA with applied field up to 9000 Oe and reached plateau at12000 Oe. The net magnetization value was detected to be 55 emu g−1. The elevated magnetization and superparamagnetic nature support the magnetic receptor as an efficient platform for Hg removal in presence of applied external field. In addition to the fluorescence sensing of Hg our probe can be used for magnetic removal of Hg. Different concentration of probe and Hg was taken as in Table 1 . After incubation period, the precipitate of γFe2O3-DMSA-Hg was isolated magnetically and assayed using AAS technique (Scheme 2 ) [42]. From AAS data the Hg removal efficiency of our probe was calculated using formula E%= [(Sin-Sout)/ Sin]* 100. It was observed that our probe could remove up to 70% of Hg from aqueous sample.

Tape water and river water were collected and spiked with standard Hg solutions to justify the practicability of this sensor. Water sample without Hg is considered as reference. The standardization curve was outlined for each aqueous sample spiked with analyte solution and represented in Figs. S8, S9. Considering a concentration range of analyte, the recovery percentage was calculated as 87% and 76% for tape and river water respectively. This indicates the present probe has good precision and can be utilized for the detection of Hg in real aqueous sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg detection in bacteria cells and fish samples
ce:section.ce:para: Mercury after a long period transformed to methyl mercury by microbes and other bioprocesses. Fish soak up methyl mercury from their nutriment and water as it passes over their gills. Mercury is compactly tied to proteins in fish tissue mainly in viscera and muscles. So, bioaccumulation of mercury in fish and sea food carries over to human population where it can result mercury poisoning. In order to avoid eco-toxicological risk factor, it is very important to monitor mercury in fish samples. Here we intended to investigate efficacy of our probe towards recognition of bio-accumulated mercury.

In summary, we have synthesized an integrated nanoprobe consisting of γ-Fe2O3 nanocrystals and fluorescent CQD for selective and perceptive detection of Hg in aqueous as well as intracellular environment. The intense emission intensity of surface passivated CQD is supressed due to PET from CQD to γ-Fe2O3. Presence of Hg positively restricts the PET mechanism between CQD-EDBE and Fe2O3-DMSA with strong enhancement in PL emission intensity of CQD. DMSA modifications on γ-Fe2O3 along with magnetic responsiveness endow the hybrid nanoparticle for magnetic removal of Hg from aqueous medium. The newly developed sensor was magnificently used to detect Hg in river and tape water and can be effectively used to monitor Hg in living fish model.
S. Panda has synthesized, characterized and done all sensing experiments under the supervision of S. Mohapatra.
B. Paital has supervised the investigation related to the detection of Hg in-vivo in fish model.
The corresponding author is responsible for ensuring that the descriptions are accurate and agreed by all authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Department of Science and Technology, INDIA (Sanction no: EMR/2014/000028).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.124445.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion
Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Nolan, S.J. Lippard, Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion, Chem. Rev. 108 (2008) 3443-3480.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Field controlled experiments on the physiological responses of maize (Zea mays L.) leaves to low-level air and soil mercury exposures
Env. Sci. Poll. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Niu, X. Zhang, S. Wang, M. Zeng, Z. Wang, Y. Zhang, Z. Ci, Field controlled experiments on the physiological responses of maize (Zea mays L.) leaves to low-level air and soil mercury exposures, Env. Sci. Poll. Res. 21 (2014) 1541-1547.
